overall_assessment: Acceptable corrected baseline with no blocking elegance regression inside the bounded RR-AUTH-04 package. Remaining issues are bounded structural debt already consistent with the round-01 accepted-debt posture.
recommended_path: Keep the package closed from an elegance perspective, record the residual findings as non-blocking debt, and avoid reopening RR-AUTH-04 for refactor-only cleanup.

findings.title: Reset issuance and consume-followed-by-mutation orchestration still lives in two profile services
findings.rationale: TenantProfileService and LandlordProfileService still each carry the same high-level password-reset choreography: lookup, missing-user work-factor absorption, per-user cooldown handling, issuance, token consumption, and release-on-failure recovery. The corrected baseline is behaviorally aligned and well covered, but the canonical reset flow still depends on two application-layer implementations staying synchronized, which leaves a real future-drift seam if another hardening pass touches only one side.
findings.suggested_action: When a future auth slice legitimately reopens this surface, establish a shared application reset orchestrator above PasswordResetTokenService so tenant and landlord flows delegate to one canonical sequence.

findings.title: The tenant public password-route guardrail still validates source text rather than route metadata
findings.rationale: The corrected baseline now enforces the required password middleware through architecture_guardrails.php, but the check still uses exact regexes over public_tenant_maybe_api_v1.php route declarations. That protects the current release, yet it remains structurally brittle because benign route reshaping can break the guard without changing effective runtime intent.
findings.suggested_action: On the next auth-route inventory change, replace the source-text regex proof with a structured route inventory or runtime metadata assertion that checks middleware binding by route identity.

findings.title: PasswordResetTokenService still carries unused helper residue
findings.rationale: PasswordResetTokenService still defines private helpers normalizeTimestamp() and tokenTable() with no in-scope references. They do not create release risk, but they keep obsolete implementation shape attached to a security-sensitive service and weaken the signal of what the canonical reset-token surface actually requires.
findings.suggested_action: Remove the unused helpers the next time the token service is reopened, or add a project-level dead-private-helper check if this residue recurs in hardened security services.
